hard.next_step: 50% confidence 01 JUL - Luke has face to face meeting booked with Guru on 7th July. Workshop will not proceed, RFP to be released by July 15 30 JUN - Workshop being rescheduled, hopefully next week. Guru advised RFP will be coming in next two weeks. 12th June - Workshop scheduled for 23rd June onsite. Once completed, there will be a formal tender. 21th MAy - had broader meeting with team. July will be sent the official Tender docs. next step - 2 hour workshop to show Zycus capapbilities in mid June, then an office RFP will be sent out. Decision date has been pushed out as well to November, start implementation in Jan 2027. 11th MAy: spoke to contact and received confirmation we will be receiving tender docs by the end of this month. 10th April: Waiting on tender documents. 31st March: Spoke to contact, looking at going to market by end of April to be released. Most likely make a decision in July now as requirements gathering is taking longer than expected. 27th March: Still waiting tender docs. 13th March: Awaiting tender docs. provided a really solid demo and presentation top key stakeholders. Reason we are confident, is we have the best vendor to connect and understand priorities and build relationships, also provided our RFP template for them to use. 26th Feb: Completed onsite presentation/demo, met key stakeholders. Next step is to participate in their official tender coming in March. 12th Feb: Demo confirmed for 20th Feb. 10th Feb: Onsite demo has been pushed to end of Feb as main stakeholders were sick on the day. 22nd Jan: Onsite demo scheduled for 4th Feb in Melbourne with the entire procurement team. Post this, a formal RFP will be considered and distributed to vendors. 14th Jan no updates and awaiting for mid level contacts to return to work 5th Jan: Still on leave. Scheduled to talk mid Jan. 12th Dec: Decision to postpone the demo to his entire Procurement and Finance Team to end of Jan 2026. 29th November - met on site on the 27th Nov, next step demo onsite with his team - full S2P process with his entire team. 6th Nov- email sent to check status 4th September 2025 Vladimir Barisic (Manager, Digital & Enterprise Procurement) connected with Adam (Sales Director). He sent an EOI for Source to Contract. Submission date is October 7th, 2025
hard.ais_why:
• Across 1,672 chars of NextStep covering 13 dated entries from Sep 2025 through May 2026 ‚Äî including EOI submission, onsite demo, stakeholder presentation, and tender wait ‚Äî zero buyer-side AI mention, despite Agentic AI and ANA being in product scope. On 13 Mar 2026: 'provided a really solid demo and presentation to key stakeholders. Reason we are confident, is we have the best vendor to connect and understand priorities and build relationships.' Relationship-driven positioning, not AI-driven.
• On 11 May 2026: 'spoke to contact and received confirmation we will be receiving tender docs by the end of this month.' On 26 Feb 2026: 'Completed onsite presentation/demo, met key stakeholders. Next step is to participate in their official tender coming in March.' Vladimir Barisic (Manager, Digital & Enterprise Procurement) initiated via Adam (Sales Director). The 'Digital & Enterprise Procurement' title hints at modernisation but never crystallises into AI language in any captured note.
• Confidence is Medium. Agentic AI and ANA are in product scope and the deal has an engaged sponsor (Vladimir), but 9 months of touchpoints have produced no AI-specific buyer quote. Score 5 (boundary DOWN per rubric ‚Äî would only flip to 7 if the tender docs explicitly require AI/agentic capability).

north_star_verdict.headline: Healthy, engaged open S2C tender, but the 25 Nov 2026 close is optimistic: the RFP is not yet released (target 15 Jul, slipped from March), and AusNet's real forcing function is an Oct 2027 go-live with Ariba licenses ending Dec 2027, so no urgency to sign this year. Luke Dougherty meets Gururaj Gopalarao face-to-face in Melbourne 7 Jul to reset the relationship after Adam Hasan's departure and confirm the RFP timeline.
north_star_verdict.math: 142 days to close (25 Nov 2026). In Formal Evaluation since 23 Sep 2025 (287 days in stage). RFP repeatedly delayed (March to April to June to target 15 July 2026); 23 Jun workshop cancelled 30 Jun after the account owner Adam Hasan left Zycus; buyer received internal approval to proceed with a formal RFP on 30 Jun and will issue within 1-2 weeks. Remaining path: RFP release mid-July, vendor response + evaluation Aug-Sep, finalist selection Oct, negotiation and contracting Oct-Nov. Buyer's stated forcing function is the Oct 2027 go-live (incumbent Ariba licenses end Dec 2027), so the buyer has no compelling reason to sign by 25 Nov 2026. Close date extended once (28 Jul 2026 to 25 Nov 2026 on 20 May) to accommodate requirements-gathering; it will likely slip again into Q4 2026 or Q1 2027. Deal is genuinely live and engaged; the sole real problem is the date.
evidence: Pipeline is the honest forecast for a pre-RFP Formal Evaluation deal with no economic buyer engaged and no commercial dialogue; it is defensible and not over-forecast.

Deal is live and engaged: active email thread late June/early July, face-to-face booked 7 Jul 2026 with Gururaj Gopalarao, business case approved internally 30 Jun (Gururaj email: 'We received internal approval yesterday to proceed with a formal RFP ... issuing it market within the next 1-2 weeks').

The 25 Nov 2026 close is not credible: the RFP is not yet out and the buyer's own timeline (go-live Oct 2027, Ariba licenses end Dec 2027) points to a Q4 2026 or later decision, so the date will slip.
Continuity risk from Adam Hasan's 22 Jun departure is being managed: Carl Kimball (VP APAC) asked Luke Dougherty to personally lead the account, and both are re-engaging Gururaj and Vladimir.

metrics.narrative: AusNet's business problem is well documented but not yet quantified in dollars. Catherine Predika framed the driver on the 19 May 2026 RFP kickoff call: 'because of the energy transition, we need to get better faster in everything that we do,' and set the vision as a 'single integrated procurement supply management platform ... that covers that full life cycle of supplier engagement.' She sized the opportunity qualitatively: '$1.6 bill' spend under management (2025) across roughly 1,200 active suppliers. Gururaj Gopalarao confirmed (19 May) the capability data was 'fed into the business case, currently going through the business case review slash approval process,' which was approved internally on 30 Jun 2026. No buyer has stated a quantified ROI, savings percentage, or cycle-time target in any captured call, so the value case is qualitative today. Quantifying it (savings, cycle-time, compliance/risk exposure) is a ranked move for the RFP response and any ROI workshop.
sources: Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Catherine Predika, Gururaj Gopalarao)
SF Next_Step__c 19 May 2026
Task email 30 Jun 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao: internal approval for formal RFP)

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has engaged Zycus and none is named on any call as the budget holder. The decision sits behind an internal 'business case review slash approval process' (Gururaj Gopalarao, 19 May 2026) that cleared on 30 Jun, and Catherine Predika described endorsement being sought from 'the GMs of those individuals' who own the in-scope functions (19 May), so budget authority sits at GM/executive level above the procurement managers Zycus is talking to. The Salesforce contact roles name two candidates at that layer who have not participated: Adam Hughes (GM Procurement & Supply Chain) and Morgan Jones (GM Operations, the SF-designated primary Decision Maker). At Formal Evaluation this is a real gap, not a hygiene note. The move is to use the 7 Jul Gururaj meeting to name the business-case approver and secure an introduction before the RFP is scored.
sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Adam Hughes (GM Procurement & Supply Chain); Morgan Jones (GM Operations, Decision Maker, PRIMARY)
Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Catherine Predika, Gururaj Gopalarao)

decision_criteria.narrative: Gururaj Gopalarao stated the four scored RFP criteria explicitly on 19 May 2026: '(1) the capability and functionality of your solution ... (2) your implementation approach ... (3) the total cost of ownership ... and (4) the ease of contracting.' He tied implementation to a hard clock: 'the go live date is October one ... because our current licenses with the existing solution finishes by December 2027.' Catherine Predika added the qualitative decision drivers: a 'single integrated' platform, 'minimal customization' ('our preferred solution is the opposite of that ... we're really looking for solutions with minimal customization'), seamless SAP S/4HANA integration ('we're not changing that particular tool'), and 'AI enabled procurement technology.' Scope is Source-to-Contract plus CLM, contractor management, third-party risk and supplier network; procure-to-pay is explicitly out of scope (Sapna, 3 Jun 2026: 'requisition to pay ... is not in scope'). These are confirmed, buyer-stated criteria.
sources: Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao, Catherine Predika)
Avoma workshop-planning call 3 Jun 2026 (Sapna, Gururaj Gopalarao)

decision_process.narrative: The process is a formal, multi-stage, vendor-neutral open tender that Gururaj Gopalarao owns as the single point of contact ('Yes. I'll be the point of contact,' 19 May 2026). Sequence read from the call history: EOI (Sep 2025) to a targeted capability matrix (Q1 2026) to an onsite S2P demo (20 Feb 2026) to a pre-RFP requirements workshop (planned June, then cancelled 30 Jun) to a formal RFP (internal approval 30 Jun, issue mid-July) evaluated on the four criteria. The buyer is deliberately even-handed with the incumbent: Sapna and Gururaj confirmed on 3 Jun that Ariba is 'still in the race but treated equally ... no preferential treatment.' The workshop was explicitly non-evaluative: 'we are not going to do a formal evaluation ... to either eliminate or shortlist' (Gururaj, 3 Jun). Broad committee endorsement is required, with legal, risk, HSEQ, finance and line-of-business owners invited. Approval authority escalates to the GMs of the scope owners.
sources: Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao, Catherine Predika)
Avoma workshop-planning call 3 Jun 2026 (Sapna, Gururaj Gopalarao)
SF Next_Step_History__c Sep 2025-Jul 2026

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are visible early because AusNet made 'ease of contracting' a scored RFP criterion. Gururaj Gopalarao (19 May 2026) said AusNet will bring 'our own terms for implementation or professional services' and wants to understand each vendor's SaaS terms; Adam Hasan committed Zycus to work on the buyer's paper ('we're happy to work based on your SaaS contracts ... for both licensing and implementation'). Catherine Predika signalled contracts will be circulated early: 'we'll be circulating those earlier rather than later ... it might even be at demo phase ... Adam is our GM, he's quite keen to cover off the contractuals.' As a new-business Source-to-Contract deal (no P2P), the eventual paper is an MSA plus Order Form and an implementation SOW; a PO is not necessarily required in this market. Legal/InfoSec detail and any SI/partner role are not yet defined. Visibility will come with the RFP's contracting section.
sources: Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao, Catherine Predika, Adam Hasan)
SF Current_Contract_Expiration signal: incumbent Ariba licenses end Dec 2027 (Gururaj, 19 May 2026)

identify_pain.narrative: The confirmed pain is that AusNet's current estate cannot support its energy-transition growth: no integrated tool for contract lifecycle management ('we do not currently have a tool that is integrated into our system to handle that,' Catherine Predika, 19 May 2026), gaps in supplier prequalification, third-party and contractor risk, and safety/compliance, all sitting outside a modern platform. Catherine: 'because of the energy transition, we need to get better faster in everything that we do.' The incumbent SAP Ariba covers sourcing but not the broader S2C, CLM, risk and contractor-management scope AusNet now needs; Ariba's own upgrade path was acknowledged ('Ariba has got some technical upgrades,' Gururaj). Inferred pain (implied by the objective tender and 'minimal customization' preference) is dissatisfaction with the fragmentation and manual effort of the status quo. The pain owner is the procurement transformation team led by Catherine Predika and Vladimir Barisic.
sources: Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Catherine Predika, Gururaj Gopalarao)
Avoma workshop-planning call 3 Jun 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao)

champion.narrative: Zycus has strong access and engagement but unproven internal advocacy, which is why this reads developing rather than strong. Vladimir Barisic (Manager, Digital & Enterprise Procurement, the SF Evaluator) is the founding sponsor: he initiated the EOI via the relevant stakeholder in Sep 2025 and has answered Zycus's calls throughout ('Vlad's been great in answering our calls and helping us out,' Adam Hasan, 19 May 2026). Gururaj Gopalarao (Category and Commercial Manager) is now the operational owner and single point of contact, coordinating demo, workshop and RFP logistics, and meeting Luke Dougherty face-to-face on 7 Jul 2026. Both give access and information, but neither has been observed advocating for Zycus over Ariba: on 3 Jun Gururaj and Sapna stressed 'no preferential treatment' and an objective evaluation, so advocacy for Zycus specifically is not yet evidenced. The at-risk factor is fresh: Adam Hasan, who owned the relationship, left Zycus on 22 Jun 2026, so Luke Dougherty and Carl Kimball must re-earn the champion relationship. Building Vladimir and Gururaj into active internal advocates is a ranked move.
sources: SF OpportunityContactRole: Vladimir Barisic (Evaluator)
Avoma RFP kickoff call 19 May 2026 (Vladimir Barisic, Gururaj Gopalarao, Adam Hasan)
Avoma workshop-planning call 3 Jun 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao, Sapna)
SF Next_Step__c 01 Jul 2026; Task emails 22-26 Jun 2026 (Adam Hasan departure, Carl Kimball hand-off)

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and the only named competitor, and it remains actively in the race. Gururaj and Sapna confirmed on 3 Jun 2026 that Ariba 'is part of a solution, but they are still in the race but treated equally ... no preferential or non-preferential treatment ... no preferential treatment for them as an incumbent,' and Ariba is delivering its own 3-hour workshop on the same requirements. The competitive wedge is scope and modernity: AusNet needs integrated CLM, contractor management, third-party risk and agentic AI that Ariba does not deliver today, plus 'minimal customization' and native SAP S/4HANA integration (Zycus positions Rojo for the integration and cites 60%+ SAP customers and utility peer SEQ Water). The threat is incumbent inertia, which Adam Hasan surfaced himself on 3 Jun playing devil's advocate: 'if Ariba can do everything ... why would I go through a new tender process?' The buyer's answer was open-minded ('we might find someone ... who is going to impress'), leaving the field genuinely contestable. No new rival has surfaced since the prior sweep.
sources: SF Competitors__c: SAP Ariba
Avoma workshop-planning call 3 Jun 2026 (Gururaj Gopalarao, Sapna, Adam Hasan)
Avoma S2P demo call 19 Feb 2026 (Christoph Ernst: contracts currently in Ariba)

deal_movement.summary: Deal is progressing after a rocky June: the account owner Adam Hasan left Zycus (owner reassigned to Luke Dougherty 19 Jun), the 23 Jun workshop was cancelled, but AusNet received internal approval to proceed with a formal RFP on 30 Jun and will issue it mid-July. Last real two-way buyer touch is the live email thread into 3 Jul plus the 7 Jul face-to-face in Melbourne. Engagement is live; the close date is the concern.

Close date pushed from 28 Jul 2026 to 25 Nov 2026 (by Adam Hasan) to accommodate requirements-gathering

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and the single current competitive threat, and it remains active: Gururaj and Sapna confirmed on 3 Jun 2026 that Ariba is 'still in the race but treated equally ... no preferential treatment' and is delivering its own workshop on the same requirements. This is an open, objective tender with no buyer preference voiced for either side, so Zycus is a credible contender, not a front-runner. The wedge against Ariba is the broader integrated scope AusNet now needs (CLM, contractor management, third-party risk, agentic AI) that the incumbent does not deliver, plus native SAP S/4HANA integration and a utility peer reference (SEQ Water). The chief risk is incumbent inertia, which the buyer itself raised ('why change if Ariba can do everything?').

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: It is part of a solution, but they are still in the race but treated equally like the rest of you. There is no preferential or non preferential treatment to them.

competitors.how_we_win: Lead with integrated CLM, contractor management, third-party risk and agentic AI (Merlin, autonomous sourcing agent) that Ariba lacks; prove low switching cost via native SAP S/4HANA integration (Rojo) and the SEQ Water utility reference to neutralize incumbent inertia.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong fit on the integrated single-platform, minimal-customization, SAP-integration and AI vision; exposed on the breadth of non-core scope (demand forecasting, supply-chain management) that Zycus flagged as non-native, and on proving a low-risk Ariba replacement.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers matter most now: reach the business-case approver before the RFP scores, neutralize incumbent inertia in the RFP, and shape AusNet's scope with agentic AI before the requirements lock.
flags: Multi-thread to power now: the business case cleared executive approval on 30 Jun but Zycus has never met the approver; use the 7 Jul Gururaj meeting to name and reach Adam Hughes (GM Procurement & Supply Chain) or the scope-owning GMs before the RFP is scored.

Neutralize incumbent inertia: Ariba is treated equally and still in the race; make the RFP response lead with the integrated CLM, contractor-management, third-party-risk and agentic-AI gaps Ariba cannot fill, evidenced by the SEQ Water utility reference and native SAP S/4HANA integration.

Shape the tender before it locks: AusNet invited vendors to add capabilities beyond their requirements; seed the autonomous sourcing agent and Merlin into their scope now so the RFP criteria favour Zycus differentiation.

champion_strength.summary: Vladimir Barisic is the founding sponsor and Gururaj Gopalarao is the engaged operational contact, giving Zycus strong access and a live email cadence. But this rates developing, not strong: neither has been observed advocating for Zycus over the incumbent in an explicitly vendor-neutral tender, and the relationship owner Adam Hasan left Zycus on 22 Jun 2026, so Luke Dougherty and Carl Kimball are re-establishing trust. Missing to reach strong: evidence of internal advocacy for Zycus and a route to the economic buyer.
champion_strength.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (Evaluator); Avoma calls 19 May and 3 Jun 2026; Task emails 22-26 Jun 2026 (Adam Hasan departure, Carl Kimball hand-off)

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AusNet's vision explicitly names 'AI enabled procurement technology,' and Zycus previewed the autonomous sourcing agent and Merlin, but no buyer AI-specific requirement has crystallized and 9 months of touchpoints produced no AI buyer quote. The agentic story is under-positioned relative to the appetite and should lead the RFP response.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 5.0 / Moderate. The buyer's stated vision is 'AI enabled procurement technology' (Catherine Predika, 19 May) and asked to see AI capabilities and roadmap, but no specific AI/agentic requirement is documented and no buyer AI quote appears across the trail. Appetite is real but latent; leading with Merlin and the autonomous sourcing agent in the RFP could raise it.

Relationship continuity risk: Adam Hasan, the ANZ director who owned the account, left Zycus on 22 Jun 2026; Luke Dougherty and Carl Kimball must re-earn champion trust.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live (active email thread into July, face-to-face 7 Jul, business case approved 30 Jun), but the process is vendor-neutral and the relationship is being rebuilt after the account owner's departure, so this is a contestable, not a leading, position.

expansion_context.note: Net-new logo; no prior Closed-Won on AusNet, so executive access is not inherited and the unengaged economic buyer is a genuine gap.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: AusNet is running an objective S2C tender with SAP Ariba as the incumbent still in the race; Zycus has broad multi-thread access and an engaged operational contact in Gururaj Gopalarao, but the business-case approver is unengaged, the champion relationship is being rebuilt after Adam Hasan's exit, and the 25 Nov close will slip behind a mid-July RFP and an Oct 2027 go-live.

win_position.text: Zycus is a credible contender in an open Source-to-Contract tender: broad multi-thread access across Vladimir Barisic, Gururaj Gopalarao and Catherine Predika, a well-received onsite demo (19 Feb 2026), and a hard forcing function in the Oct 2027 go-live with Ariba licenses ending Dec 2027.

The process is explicitly vendor-neutral: Gururaj and Sapna stated on 3 Jun there is 'no preferential treatment' to the incumbent Ariba and no buyer has voiced a Zycus preference, so we are not ahead.
No economic buyer is engaged: the business case cleared executive approval on 30 Jun but Zycus has never met the approver (Adam Hughes / Morgan Jones), a real gap at this stage.

deal_momentum.text: AusNet received internal approval to proceed with a formal RFP on 30 Jun 2026 and will issue it within 1-2 weeks, restarting momentum after months of requirements-gathering delay.
Luke Dougherty meets Gururaj Gopalarao face-to-face in Melbourne on 7 Jul 2026 to reset the relationship and confirm the RFP timeline.
The RFP has slipped repeatedly (March to April to June to mid-July) and the 23 Jun workshop was cancelled after Adam Hasan's departure, so the timeline keeps stretching.

customer_commitment.text: AusNet has invested nine months (EOI Sep 2025, capability matrix Q1 2026, onsite demo Feb 2026) and named a hard Oct 2027 go-live tied to Ariba licenses ending Dec 2027.
Commitment is to buying, not to Zycus: the RFP will be scored objectively on capability, implementation approach, total cost of ownership and ease of contracting.
No pricing or commercial dialogue has occurred with a budget owner and the ROI case remains qualitative.

deal_risk.text: The 25 Nov 2026 close is not credible: with the RFP not yet released and a Oct 2027 go-live, the decision realistically lands in Q4 2026 or later.
Incumbent inertia is live: Ariba is still in the race running its own workshop, and the buyer openly asked 'why change if Ariba can do everything?'
Relationship continuity risk: Adam Hasan, who owned the account, left Zycus on 22 Jun 2026, so Luke Dougherty and Carl Kimball are re-earning the champion relationship.

champion.evidence: Access strong but advocacy unproven; account owner Adam Hasan left 22 Jun 2026.

timeline.evidence: RFP repeatedly slipped; 25 Nov close behind an Oct 2027 go-live.

critical_signals.text: SAP Ariba, the incumbent, is being evaluated equally in an objective open tender and is running its own workshop, so Zycus is a credible contender rather than the front-runner.
Luke Dougherty meets Gururaj Gopalarao face-to-face in Melbourne on 7 July to reset the relationship after the June workshop was cancelled and to confirm the mid-July RFP release.

AusNet cleared its business case internally on 30 June and will issue a formal RFP within two weeks, scoring vendors on capability, implementation, total cost of ownership and ease of contracting, with no pricing engaged yet.
AusNet is scoping intake and orchestration, contract lifecycle management, contractor management and third-party risk into the tender while keeping procure-to-pay on SAP S/4HANA out of scope.

customer_preference.evidence: Open, objective tender; Gururaj Gopalarao and Sapna stated 'no preferential treatment' to incumbent Ariba on 3 Jun 2026, and no buyer voiced a Zycus preference. Buyer stance is 'come and show us what's possible,' not a Zycus-specific lean.

business_case.evidence: Documented vision (single AI-enabled integrated S2C platform, energy-transition driver, $1.6bn spend under management, ~1,200 suppliers) and a hard Oct 2027 go-live, but no quantified ROI or savings articulated by the buyer; business case approved internally 30 Jun 2026 but numbers not shared.

items.expected_effect: Maps the economic buyer before the RFP scores, closing the biggest gap and creating a path to commercial sponsorship.

items.action: Request the draft RFP scope and criteria weighting so Zycus can pre-build a targeted response.
items.trigger: Gururaj confirmed the RFP issues within 1-2 weeks (30 Jun email).
items.expected_effect: Lets Zycus tailor the response to the four scored criteria and shape scope before it locks.

items.action: Build the RFP response leading with CLM, contractor management, third-party risk and agentic AI against Ariba's gaps.
items.trigger: Ariba remains the incumbent in an even-handed tender (3 Jun call).
items.expected_effect: Differentiates on the scope Ariba cannot fill and counters incumbent inertia.

items.action: Send Gururaj the SEQ Water utility reference and Certinal e-signature story to de-risk the Ariba replacement.
items.trigger: Buyer raised change-management and 'why change from Ariba' concerns (3 Jun).
items.expected_effect: Proves a low-risk utility migration and lowers the perceived switching cost.

items.action: Re-confirm the Oct 2027 go-live and Dec 2027 Ariba license-end as the RFP timeline anchor with Gururaj.
items.trigger: The 25 Nov 2026 close is not aligned to the buyer's real forcing function.
items.expected_effect: Realigns the forecast close date and keeps the buyer's clock working in Zycus's favour.

contributions.evidence: Verdict flags close-date risk this sweep.
Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

commentary.win_position: Win 31 is carried by momentum 62 vs stage-expected 52; champion strength +0.30 (weight 15). Offsetting it: competitive strength -1.00 (weight 15).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 62 (moving forward): commercial topics entering (from call evidence). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 0: no break-risk was observed in the swept evidence. Coverage is thin, so read this as 'nothing seen yet,' not 'nothing there.'
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 24 is anchored on the stage win (31), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 62, and dragged by risk 0. Read is Partial Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 4 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (stage integrity, exec and champion, commercial, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.intro: AusNet is running an objective S2C tender with SAP Ariba as the incumbent still in the race; Zycus has broad multi-thread access and an engaged operational contact in Gururaj Gopalarao, but the business-case approver is unengaged, the champion relationship is being rebuilt after Adam Hasan's exit, and the 25 Nov close will slip behind a mid-July RFP and an Oct 2027 go-live

bullets.text: Zycus is a credible contender in an open Source-to-Contract tender; broad multi-thread access across Vladimir Barisic, Gururaj Gopalarao and Catherine Predika, a well-received onsite demo (19 Feb 2026), and a hard forcing function in the Oct 2027 go-live with Ariba licenses ending Dec 2027
The process is explicitly vendor-neutral; Gururaj and Sapna stated on 3 Jun there is 'no preferential treatment' to the incumbent Ariba and no buyer has voiced a Zycus preference, so we are not ahead
No economic buyer is engaged; the business case cleared executive approval on 30 Jun but Zycus has never met the approver (Adam Hughes / Morgan Jones), a real gap at this stage
AusNet received internal approval to proceed with a formal RFP on 30 Jun 2026 and will issue it within 1-2 weeks, restarting momentum after months of requirements-gathering delay
Luke Dougherty meets Gururaj Gopalarao face-to-face in Melbourne on 7 Jul 2026 to reset the relationship and confirm the RFP timeline
The RFP has slipped repeatedly (March to April to June to mid-July) and the 23 Jun workshop was cancelled after Adam Hasan's departure, so the timeline keeps stretching

blocks.title: What could lose it
blocks.read: The threat is the deal stalling into 'do nothing', not a competitor beating us.

bullets.text: The 25 Nov 2026 close is not credible; with the RFP not yet released and a Oct 2027 go-live, the decision realistically lands in Q4 2026 or later
Incumbent inertia is live; Ariba is still in the race running its own workshop, and the buyer openly asked 'why change if Ariba can do everything?'
Relationship continuity risk; Adam Hasan, who owned the account, left Zycus on 22 Jun 2026, so Luke Dougherty and Carl Kimball are re-earning the champion relationship

blocks.footer: Competitive picture: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and the single current competitive threat, and it remains active: Gururaj and Sapna confirmed on 3 Jun 2026 that Ariba is 'still in the race but treated equally ... no preferential treatment' and is delivering…

blocks.title: Customer commitment
blocks.read: Light — they haven't committed much yet.

bullets.text: AusNet has invested nine months (EOI Sep 2025, capability matrix Q1 2026, onsite demo Feb 2026) and named a hard Oct 2027 go-live tied to Ariba licenses ending Dec 2027
Commitment is to buying, not to Zycus; the RFP will be scored objectively on capability, implementation approach, total cost of ownership and ease of contracting
No pricing or commercial dialogue has occurred with a budget owner and the ROI case remains qualitative

blocks.title: What moves it forward
items: At the 7 Jul Melbourne meeting, ask Gururaj to name the business-case approver and arrange an intro. (by 7 Jul)
Request the draft RFP scope and criteria weighting so Zycus can pre-build a targeted response. (by 13 Jul)
Build the RFP response leading with CLM, contractor management, third-party risk and agentic AI against Ariba's gaps. (by 22 Jul)

evidence_coverage.salesforce_window: activities 120d; field history 365d; contact roles full
gaps: Economic buyer / business-case approver not identified or engaged (knowledge gap).
No quantified value case (savings, cycle-time, risk exposure) shared by the buyer.
Only one competitor (SAP Ariba) named across all sources; other RFP entrants not yet identified.
